---
title: "Flow Matching and Transport Views of Generation"
description: "A bridge page showing how flow matching learns a time-dependent velocity field that transports noise into data, and why this gives a transport-based alternative to diffusion-style generation."
page-type: application
status: published
update-status: current
level: bridge
topic: machine-learning
prerequisites:
  - /applications/machine-learning/diffusion-models-and-denoising.qmd
  - /applications/machine-learning/score-matching-and-the-sde-view-of-diffusion.qmd
  - /applications/machine-learning/optimization-for-machine-learning.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Explain generation as transport from a simple source distribution to a data distribution."
  - "Interpret flow matching as learning a velocity field along a probability path."
  - "Recognize the difference between ODE transport views and reverse-SDE diffusion views."
  - "Understand why straighter transport paths can support faster sampling."
applications:
  - machine-learning
  - generative-modeling
  - deep-learning
keywords:
  - flow matching
  - transport
  - velocity field
  - ODE
  - rectified flow
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-24
source-check-date: 2026-04-24
review-cadence: semiannual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
---

## Application Snapshot

The diffusion story says:

- corrupt data with noise
- learn how to reverse the corruption

The flow-matching story says:

- start from a simple source such as Gaussian noise
- define a path from source to data
- learn the velocity field that moves points along that path

So generation becomes a transport problem:

`learn how to push a simple distribution into the data distribution`

This gives a clean bridge from generative modeling to differential equations, transport, and geometry.

## Problem Setting

Let $x_0$ be a source sample, often drawn from a simple distribution such as a Gaussian, and let $x_1$ be a target data sample.

Choose a time-dependent probability path $x_t$ for $t \in [0,1]$ that moves from source to target.

Flow matching trains a model $v_\theta(x,t)$ to approximate the velocity field that should move points along that path:

$$
\frac{dx_t}{dt} = v(x_t,t).
$$

After training, we generate by solving the learned ODE

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} = v_\theta(x,t)
$$

starting from a simple source sample and integrating forward toward data.

So instead of reversing a noising process step by step, we learn a transport field directly.

## Why This Math Appears

This page continues the site's generative sequence:

- [Diffusion Models and Denoising](diffusion-models-and-denoising.qmd): generation as reversing gradual corruption
- [Score Matching and the SDE View of Diffusion](score-matching-and-the-sde-view-of-diffusion.qmd): generation as reverse-time stochastic dynamics guided by a learned field
- [Optimization for Machine Learning](optimization-for-machine-learning.qmd): the velocity field is still learned by gradient-based training

The new mathematical move is:

`replace reverse stochastic denoising with direct learning of a transport vector field`

That is why words such as `flow`, `velocity`, `trajectory`, and `transport path` show up.

## Math Objects In Use

- source sample $x_0$
- target sample $x_1$
- interpolation or probability path $x_t$
- velocity field $v(x,t)$
- learned vector field $v_\theta(x,t)$
- transport ODE
- sampling trajectory from source to data

## A Small Worked Walkthrough

Take the simplest possible one-dimensional path.

Let

$$
x_0 = 0,
\qquad
x_1 = 2.
$$

Choose the straight interpolation

$$
x_t = (1-t)x_0 + t x_1 = 2t.
$$

Then the time derivative is

$$
\frac{dx_t}{dt} = 2.
$$

So along this path, the correct velocity field is just the constant

$$
v(x_t,t)=2.
$$

If a model learns that velocity exactly, then starting from $x(0)=0$ and solving

$$
\frac{dx}{dt}=2
$$

gives

$$
x(t)=2t,
\qquad
x(1)=2.
$$

This toy case is deliberately simple, but it shows the whole logic:

- choose a path from source to target
- compute the target velocity along that path
- train a model to predict that velocity from $(x_t,t)$
- generate by integrating the learned field

In high dimensions, the same story holds, except the path and the velocity field live in a much larger space and are represented by a neural network.

## Why This Is Related to Diffusion

Flow matching is not disconnected from diffusion. In fact, the connection is one reason the topic matters.

Both families learn time-dependent fields over intermediate distributions between noise and data.

The difference is mostly in the lens:

- `diffusion / score-based view`: learn the field needed to reverse a stochastic noising process
- `flow-matching view`: learn the velocity field that transports mass along a chosen path

The original flow-matching paper also shows that suitable Gaussian probability paths can recover diffusion-like paths as special cases.

So flow matching is not an unrelated generative universe. It is part of the same broader movement toward path-based generative modeling.

## Implementation or Computation Note

Why do people care about flow matching so much?

Because the transport view can make sampling simpler and faster.

If the learned path is relatively straight and the velocity field is well behaved, then numerical ODE integration may require fewer function evaluations than long reverse-diffusion chains.

That is why later work often emphasizes:

- straighter paths
- optimal-transport-inspired couplings
- rectified flow
- efficient ODE solvers

The practical design choices now become:

- which source distribution to use
- which probability path to use
- how to define pairings or couplings between source and target
- how to parameterize and integrate the velocity field

This is where flow matching becomes both a modeling question and a numerical-method question.

## Failure Modes

- thinking flow matching is just a renamed normalizing flow
- assuming every flow-matching model learns exact optimal transport
- ignoring that path choice strongly affects training and sampling behavior
- treating deterministic ODE transport and stochastic diffusion as if they were identical
- forgetting that faster sampling depends on the geometry of the learned trajectories, not only on the model size
- overclaiming from toy straight-line intuition to all real generative problems

## Paper Bridge

- [Flow Matching for Generative Modeling](https://openreview.net/forum?id=PqvMRDCJT9t) - `First pass` - the core paper introducing flow matching as supervised learning of vector fields along probability paths. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Rectified Flow: A Marginal Preserving Approach to Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.14577) - `Paper bridge` - influential transport-facing paper showing why straighter trajectories can matter for efficient generation. Checked `2026-04-24`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [CME296: Diffusion and Large Vision Models](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/2276231) - `First pass` - official Stanford course listing explicitly grouping diffusion, score matching, and flow matching in one modern generative curriculum. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Flow Matching for Generative Modeling](https://openreview.net/forum?id=PqvMRDCJT9t) - `First pass` - primary source for the flow-matching framework. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Flow Matching Guide and Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.06264) - `Second pass` - broad guide for the modern flow-matching ecosystem once the basic velocity-field idea is clear. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Rectified Flow: A Marginal Preserving Approach to Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.14577) - `Second pass` - primary source for the straighter-transport viewpoint that now strongly shapes fast generators. Checked `2026-04-24`.
- [Stochastic Interpolants: A Unifying Framework for Flows and Diffusions](https://jmlr.org/papers/v26/23-1605.html) - `Paper bridge` - useful unifying view when you want one mathematical language covering both deterministic transport and stochastic generative paths. Checked `2026-04-24`.
